class-files-and-instances.label: Class Files and Instances
class-files-and-instances.summary: Create a class in its own file, import it, and make separate objects from it.

sketch0.title: Put a class in its own workspace file

sketch1.title: Use __init__ to give each instance its own state

sketch2.title: Import the class and call methods on different objects

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch0.title: Create a class in a model file
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch0.prompt: A library app should keep its `Book` class separate from the code that uses it. Complete the workspace so `models/book.py` defines a `Book` class with an `__init__(self, title, pages)` constructor that stores `title` and `pages`. Then update the main code so it creates `Book("Deep Work", 320)` and prints the title on one line and the page count on the next line.
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch0.hint: Read the task "Create a class in a model file" and identify the required result.

help.concept: This coding exercise checks whether your code produces the requested result for: "Create a class in a model file".
help.hint_1: Use the statement or expression that matches the required behavior in the code editor.
help.hint_2: Click Run and compare the output panel with the expected result.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch0.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.book import Book

# Create one Book object below.
# Print its title, then print its pages.

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

# Create one Book object below.
# Print its title, then print its pages.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.title: Store constructor values on separate instances
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.prompt: A reading club wants two separate `Book` objects. In `models/book.py`, finish the `Book` constructor so each object stores its own `title` and `pages`. In the main code, create `Book("Dune", 412)` and `Book("Emma", 300)`, then print the first book's title and the second book's page count.
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.hint: Read the task "Store constructor values on separate instances" and identify the required result.

help.concept: This coding exercise checks whether your code produces the requested result for: "Store constructor values on separate instances".
help.hint_1: Use the statement or expression that matches the required behavior in the code editor.
help.hint_2: Click Run and compare the output panel with the expected result.

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch1.starterCode:

from models.book import Book

first_book = Book("Dune", 412)
second_book = Book("Emma", 300)

# Print the first book's title.

first_book = Book("Dune", 412)
second_book = Book("Emma", 300)

try_class_files_and_instances_sketch2.title: Add and call an instance method
try_class_files_and_instances_sketch2.prompt: A library dashboard needs a short description for each book. In `models/book.py`, add a `summary(self)` method to `Book` that returns text in this format: `Title: <title>, Pages: <pages>`. In the main code, create `Book("Beloved", 275)` and print the result of calling `summary()`.

mc-class-vs-instance.title: Class or instance?
mc-class-vs-instance.prompt: Which statements are correct about classes and instances in this topic? Choose all that apply.
mc-class-vs-instance.hint: One choice describes the blueprint, and another describes the created object.

help.concept: A class defines structure and behavior, while an instance is one object created from that class.
help.hint_1: Look for the option that describes a reusable definition and the option that describes a specific created object.
help.hint_2: Reject choices that say every object shares one set of attribute values automatically or that imports create objects by themselves.

options.a: A class is a blueprint for creating objects.
options.b: An instance is a specific object created from a class.
options.c: Importing a class automatically creates all objects you need.
options.d: Two instances from one class must always have identical attribute values.

fb-init-self-title.title: Complete an attribute assignment
fb-init-self-title.prompt: Fill in the missing value so the constructor stores the incoming title on the object.
fb-init-self-title.hint: The right side should use the parameter, not the object name.

help.concept: Inside `__init__`, you usually copy incoming parameter values onto `self` attributes.
help.hint_1: The left side names the attribute on the object. The blank is the constructor parameter being stored.
help.hint_2: Use the parameter that already holds the incoming book the missing term, not `self` and not `print`.

dr-import-order.title: Order the steps to use a class from another workspace file
dr-import-order.prompt: Put these steps in the correct order for using a class stored in its own workspace file.
dr-import-order.hint: You need the class definition before you can import it and create objects from it.

help.concept: A typical workflow is define the class, import it, create an instance, then use its data or methods.
help.hint_1: Start with the class existing in its model file, not with printing.
help.hint_2: After the class exists, bring it into the main code, create an object, and only then read from it.

tokens.t1: Define the class in a model file
tokens.t2: Import the class into the main code
tokens.t3: Create an instance with constructor values
tokens.t4: Print an attribute or method result

project-step-account-class.title: Project step: create the Account class
project-step-account-class.prompt: Start an account tracker. Define an `Account` class in `models/account.py` with an `__init__(self, owner, balance)` constructor that stores `owner` and `balance`. In the main code, create `Account("Ava", 120)` and print the owner on one line and the balance on the next line.
project-step-account-class.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

from models.account import Account

# Create Ava's account and print owner then balance.

main_py.content:

project-step-account-methods.title: Project step: add account methods
project-step-account-methods.prompt: Build on the account tracker. Keep the `Account` class with `owner` and `balance`, then add a `deposit(self, amount)` method that increases the balance and a `summary(self)` method that returns text in this format: `<owner>: $<balance>`. In the main code, create `Account("Ava", 120)`, call `deposit(30)`, and print the summary.
project-step-account-methods.hint: One method changes state, and the other reports the current state.

help.concept: This step checks that methods can both update object state and return text based on the updated attributes.
help.hint_1: `deposit` should add the incoming amount to `self.balance`.
help.hint_2: After calling `deposit(30)`, the summary should reflect the new balance, not the original one.

project-step-account-methods.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.account import Account

account = Account("Ava", 120)

# Deposit 30, then print the summary.

main_py.content:
from models.account import Account

account = Account("Ava", 120)

# Deposit 30, then print the summary.

project-step-two-accounts.title: Project step: track two separate accounts
project-step-two-accounts.prompt: Continue the account tracker from the previous step. Keep the `Account` class with `owner`, `balance`, `deposit`, and `summary`. In the main code, create `Account("Ava", 120)` and `Account("Noah", 90)`. Call `deposit(30)` on Ava's account only. Then print Ava's summary and Noah's summary on separate lines so the output shows that each object keeps its own state.
project-step-two-accounts.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

project-step-two-accounts.starterCode:
# main.py
from models.account import Account

ava_account = Account("Ava", 120)
noah_account = Account("Noah", 90)

# Deposit 30 into Ava's account only.
# Print Ava's summary, then Noah's summary.

ava_account = Account("Ava", 120)
noah_account = Account("Noah", 90)

mc-import-benefits.title: Why keep the class in its own workspace file?
mc-import-benefits.prompt: Why is it useful to keep a class like `Account` or `Book` in its own workspace file and import it into the main code? Choose all that apply.
mc-import-benefits.hint: Think about reuse and keeping responsibilities organized.

help.concept: Separating a class into its own workspace file makes the code easier to reuse and keeps the model logic organized.
help.hint_1: Look for answers about organization and reuse, not answers about automatic object creation.
help.hint_2: A separate class file helps you import the same model in different parts of a project and keeps object behavior grouped together.

options.a: It keeps the model definition organized in one place.
options.b: It makes the class reusable from other parts of the project.
options.c: It forces every instance to share the same attribute values.
options.d: It removes the need to create objects with the constructor.

fb-import-book.title: Complete the import statement
fb-import-book.prompt: Fill in the missing class name so the main code can import the model correctly.
fb-import-book.hint: The imported name should match the class defined in the model.

help.concept: An import statement must name the class you want to bring into the current code.
help.hint_1: The module path already points to `models.the missing term`. The blank is the class name.
help.hint_2: Use the capitalized class name from the topic examples, not the lowercase module name.

sc-self-meaning.title: What does self refer to?
sc-self-meaning.prompt: Inside a class method like `__init__` or `summary`, what does `self` refer to?
sc-self-meaning.hint: It is not the class itself and not every object at once.

help.concept: In instance methods, `self` refers to the specific object the method is working with.
help.hint_1: Think about how `self.title` can be different for two objects from the same class.
help.hint_2: `self` points to the current instance whose method was called.

options.a: The specific instance using the method
options.b: The import statement for the class
options.c: Every instance in the whole program at once
options.d: A special keyword that only works in print statements

sketch-0.title: Put a class in its own workspace file
sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
A class often belongs in its own workspace file so the rest of your program can import and use it.

For example, a small reading tracker might keep its `Book` class in a model file and create objects in another file:

```python
from models.book import Book

first_book = Book("Deep Work", 320)
second_book = Book("Atomic Habits", 280)

print(first_book.title)
print(second_book.pages)
```

Line by line:

- `from models.book import Book` brings the `Book` class into the current code.
- `first_book = Book("Deep Work", 320)` creates one instance.
- `second_book = Book("Atomic Habits", 280)` creates a different instance.
- `print(first_book.title)` reads data from the first object.
- `print(second_book.pages)` reads data from the second object.

The important idea is that the class is the blueprint, and each object made from it stores its own data.

sketch-1.title: Use __init__ to give each instance its own state
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
The `__init__` method runs when you create an object. It is where you usually store starting values on `self`.

Example:

```python
class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, pages):
        self.title = title
        self.pages = pages
```

Step by step:

- `class Book:` starts the class definition.
- `def __init__(self, title, pages):` defines the constructor.
- `self` means the specific object being created.
- `self.title = title` stores the incoming title on that object.
- `self.pages = pages` stores the incoming page count on that object.

If you create two books, each one gets its own `title` and `pages` values. That is why instances can behave like separate things even though they come from the same class.

sketch-2.title: Import the class and call methods on different objects
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
Methods let an object do something with its own state.

Here is a simple example:

```python
class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, pages):
        self.title = title
        self.pages = pages

    def summary(self):
        return f"{self.title} has {self.pages} pages"
```

And then another part of the program can use it:

```python
from models.book import Book

morning_read = Book("Deep Work", 320)
evening_read = Book("Atomic Habits", 280)

print(morning_read.summary())
print(evening_read.summary())
```

Line by line:

- The class defines a method named `summary`.
- `return f"{self.title} has {self.pages} pages"` builds text from that object's attributes.
- `morning_read.summary()` calls the method on the first object.
- `evening_read.summary()` calls the same method on the second object.

The same class method can produce different results because each instance has different stored values.
